name: North Macedonia
tagline: From the heart of ancient Macedon to a young Balkan republic that had to win the right to its own name.
summary: The Republic of North Macedonia occupies the northern, 'Vardar' part of the wider geographic region of Macedonia, a crossroads whose history has been layered, contested and shared. The land was the cradle of the ancient kingdom of Macedon, home of Philip II and Alexander the Great, though the relationship of the modern Slavic state to that classical heritage is itself disputed. Roman and then Byzantine rule gave way, from the sixth century, to Slavic settlement; in the early Middle Ages the region became the centre of Tsar Samuel's Bulgarian state, with its capital at Ohrid and a celebrated Slavonic literary school founded by Saints Clement and Naum. Five centuries of Ottoman rule ended only with the Balkan Wars of 1912–13, which partitioned Macedonia among Serbia, Greece and Bulgaria, the Vardar portion passing to Serbia and, after 1945, becoming a republic of Tito's Yugoslavia, where the Macedonian language and nation were codified. The country declared independence peacefully in 1991, but its path was blocked for decades by a dispute with Greece over its very name, settled only by the 2018 Prespa Agreement, which renamed it the Republic of North Macedonia and opened the door to NATO membership in 2020. A 2001 ethnic Albanian insurgency was ended by the Ohrid Framework Agreement, and a continuing identity dispute with Bulgaria still complicates its bid to join the European Union.

founding.label: Peaceful independence from Yugoslavia
founding.detail: On 8 September 1991 the citizens of the Socialist Republic of Macedonia voted overwhelmingly, in a referendum approved by some 96 percent of those taking part, for a sovereign and independent state. Under President Kiro Gligorov the republic seceded from the disintegrating Yugoslav federation peacefully, the only one to do so without war or ethnic cleansing. Yet full international recognition was delayed for years by Greece's objection to the name 'Macedonia', which it regarded as implying a claim on its own northern province and on the heritage of ancient Macedon. The country was admitted to the United Nations in 1993 under the provisional designation 'the former Yugoslav Republic of Macedonia', a name it carried until the dispute was finally resolved by the Prespa Agreement of 2018 and the country was renamed the Republic of North Macedonia.

quickFacts.label: Ancient heartland
quickFacts.value: The Vardar region lay within the ancient kingdom of Macedon of Philip II and Alexander the Great

quickFacts.label: Medieval centre
quickFacts.value: Capital of Tsar Samuel's Bulgarian state at Ohrid (reigned to 1014), seat of a famed Slavonic literary school

quickFacts.label: Partition of Macedonia
quickFacts.value: Divided among Serbia, Greece and Bulgaria after the Balkan Wars of 1912–13

quickFacts.label: Independence
quickFacts.value: Peaceful secession from Yugoslavia by referendum, 8 September 1991

quickFacts.label: The name dispute
quickFacts.value: Resolved by the Prespa Agreement with Greece (17 June 2018); renamed the Republic of North Macedonia, 2019

quickFacts.label: NATO membership
quickFacts.value: Joined NATO as its 30th member on 27 March 2020

eras.title: Ancient Macedon and the Classical Legacy
eras.standfirst: The wider region gave its name to the kingdom of Philip II and Alexander the Great, then passed under Rome and Byzantium, even as the modern state's claim on that ancient inheritance remains contested.
body: The name Macedonia belongs first to antiquity. In the classical age a kingdom of Macedon rose on the periphery of the Greek world and, under Philip II (reigned 359–336 BCE), came to dominate the Greek city-states. Philip reformed the army, defeated Athens and Thebes at Chaeronea and forged the Greeks into the League of Corinth; his son Alexander the Great then carried Macedonian arms across the Persian empire to the borders of India, spreading Hellenistic culture from Egypt to Central Asia. The territory of the modern republic lies in the northern, inland part of the wider geographic region of Macedonia, the 'Vardar' portion drained by the Vardar (Axios) river; the relationship of today's Slavic-speaking state to the ancient kingdom is itself a matter of dispute, and the Prespa Agreement of 2018 would later draw an explicit line between the two.

After defeating the last Macedonian pretender near Pydna in 148 BCE, Rome absorbed Macedonia as its fifth province. For centuries the region prospered as part of the Roman and then the Eastern Roman (Byzantine) world, crossed by the Via Egnatia, the great road linking the Adriatic to Constantinople. Towns such as Ohrid (ancient Lychnidos) and Scupi, near modern Skopje, were Roman and early Christian centres; Scupi was levelled by a catastrophic earthquake in 518 CE.

Byzantine administration endured here in one form or another into the late medieval period, but from the sixth century the demographic character of the interior began to change profoundly as Slavic peoples moved south across the Danube and settled the Balkan interior. The classical and Roman city life of the region gave way, over the following centuries, to a new Slavic-speaking population whose arrival set the stage for the medieval history of the land.

events.title: Reign of Philip II of Macedon
events.summary: Philip II reformed the Macedonian army and won mastery over the Greek city-states at Chaeronea, laying the foundations for the empire his son Alexander the Great would build across Asia.

events.title: Conquests of Alexander the Great
events.summary: Alexander III led Macedonian and Greek forces in the conquest of the Persian empire, carrying Hellenistic civilization from Egypt to the Indus before his death at Babylon in 323 BCE.

events.title: Macedonia becomes a Roman province
events.summary: After Rome defeated the last Macedonian claimant near Pydna, Macedonia was organised as a Roman province, beginning centuries of Roman and later Byzantine rule over the region.

events.title: Destruction of Scupi
events.summary: A devastating earthquake destroyed the Roman city of Scupi near present-day Skopje, an event marking the late-antique decline of the region's classical urban life on the eve of Slavic settlement.

figures.name: Philip II of Macedon
figures.blurb: The 18th king of Macedon, Philip II transformed a peripheral kingdom into the dominant power of Greece, reforming the army and defeating Athens and Thebes at Chaeronea, laying the foundations for his son's conquests.

figures.name: Alexander the Great
figures.blurb: Son of Philip II, Alexander III conquered the Persian empire and carried Hellenistic culture across the ancient world, one of history's most celebrated commanders and an enduring symbol over whose legacy modern states later contended.

pullquote.text: The modern republic occupies the northern, 'Vardar' part of the wider region of Macedonia, and its relationship to the ancient kingdom of Philip and Alexander is itself contested.
pullquote.attribution: On the layered meaning of the name Macedonia

eras.title: Slavic Settlement, Tsar Samuel and the Ohrid Archbishopric
eras.standfirst: Slavs settled the region, which became the heart of a Bulgarian state centred on Ohrid, a city whose Slavonic literary school and archbishopric made it a cradle of medieval Slavic Christianity.
body: From the sixth and seventh centuries Slavic tribes settled across the Macedonian interior, gradually displacing the Romano-Byzantine order. In the ninth century most of the region was drawn into the First Bulgarian Empire, and it was here that one of the formative achievements of Slavic civilization took root. Around 886 Saint Clement of Ohrid, a disciple of Saints Cyril and Methodius, established a literary school at Ohrid; with Saint Naum he taught a Slavonic liturgy and script, training clergy and copyists and helping to spread the Old Church Slavonic letters and Christian learning among the Slavs. Ohrid became one of the oldest and most important spiritual centres of the Slavic world.

When the First Bulgarian Empire fragmented under Byzantine pressure, its western lands rallied around a new ruler. Samuel (reigned to 1014) built a powerful state in the western Balkans and made Ohrid his capital, where he also established a patriarchate. For a generation his realm resisted the relentless campaigns of the Byzantine emperor Basil II. The struggle ended in catastrophe: after the battle of Kleidion in 1014 Basil, remembered as the 'Bulgar-slayer', broke Samuel's army, and the tsar died soon afterwards. By 1018 the whole region had been reincorporated into the Byzantine Empire.

Byzantine reconquest did not extinguish Ohrid's ecclesiastical importance. The autocephalous Archbishopric of Ohrid, established after 1018, remained a major centre of Orthodox church life and Slavonic culture for centuries, its churches and monasteries adorned with Byzantine frescoes and icons. The medieval town of Ohrid and the lake beside it — one of the oldest human settlements in Europe and home to the monastery of Saint Naum — would later be inscribed by UNESCO as a site of outstanding natural and cultural heritage.

events.title: Slavic settlement of the region
events.summary: Slavic tribes migrated into the Macedonian interior from the sixth century, transforming the population of the region and laying the basis for its later Slavic-speaking identity.

events.title: The Ohrid literary school of Clement and Naum
events.summary: Saint Clement of Ohrid, with Saint Naum, founded a Slavonic literary school at Ohrid, spreading the Slavic script and Christian learning and making the city a cradle of medieval Slavic culture.

events.title: Tsar Samuel's state centred on Ohrid
events.summary: Samuel built a powerful western Bulgarian state with its capital and a patriarchate at Ohrid, resisting Byzantine expansion for decades until his defeat by the emperor Basil II.

events.title: Battle of Kleidion and the fall of Samuel
events.summary: The Byzantine emperor Basil II destroyed Samuel's army at Kleidion; the tsar died shortly afterwards, and by 1018 the region was fully reabsorbed into the Byzantine Empire.

events.title: The Archbishopric of Ohrid
events.summary: After the Byzantine reconquest an autocephalous archbishopric was established at Ohrid, sustaining the city's role as a centre of Orthodox church life and Slavonic learning.

figures.name: Saint Clement of Ohrid
figures.blurb: A disciple of Saints Cyril and Methodius, Clement established the literary school at Ohrid that spread the Slavic alphabet and Christian learning, becoming one of the founders of medieval Slavic literature and the region's patron saint.

figures.name: Tsar Samuel
figures.role: Ruler of the western Bulgarian state
figures.blurb: Samuel made Ohrid the capital and seat of a patriarchate of a powerful Balkan state that resisted the Byzantine emperor Basil II for decades, until his defeat at Kleidion in 1014 and death soon after.

pullquote.text: Following a revolt of the western provinces, Samuel set up his own capital in Ohrid and also established a patriarchate there.
pullquote.attribution: Encyclopaedia Britannica, on the medieval centre at Ohrid

eras.title: Ottoman Rule and the Macedonian Question
eras.standfirst: Five centuries of Ottoman rule ended in a contest among neighbouring nations and competing revolutionaries, dramatised by the Ilinden Uprising of 1903 and the short-lived Kruševo Republic.
body: Ottoman armies conquered the region in the late fourteenth century, and for some five hundred years Macedonia formed part of the Ottoman Empire, its name even disappearing for a time as an administrative designation. The population was a patchwork of Orthodox Slavs, Greeks, Albanians, Turks, Vlachs, Jews and others. As Ottoman power waned in the nineteenth century and new Balkan nation-states emerged around it, the still-Ottoman region of Macedonia became the object of rival claims by Serbia, Greece and Bulgaria, each promoting its own church, schools and national programme. This contest over the land and the loyalties of its people became known as the 'Macedonian Question'.

Out of this ferment came the Internal Macedonian Revolutionary Organization (IMRO), a secret society founded in 1893 at Salonika. Its early leaders, among them Gotse Delchev, were men who combined a Macedonian regional identity with, for many, a Bulgarian national one, and the movement was torn between two goals: an autonomous Macedonia and union with Bulgaria. On 2 August 1903, St Elijah's Day, IMRO launched the Ilinden Uprising against Ottoman rule. In the town of Kruševo the insurgents proclaimed a short-lived republic, which survived only about ten days before Ottoman forces overran it.

The uprising was crushed within weeks amid harsh reprisals that drove tens of thousands into flight, but it left an enduring mark. Ilinden became a founding myth of Macedonian national feeling, commemorated to this day, even as Serbs, Greeks and Bulgarians continued to dispute the identity of the region's Slavs. The 'Macedonian Question' would be settled not by local revolution but, as events proved, by the armies and diplomacy of the surrounding states.

events.title: Ottoman conquest of the region
events.summary: Ottoman forces conquered Macedonia in the late fourteenth century, beginning roughly five centuries of Ottoman rule during which the region's name lapsed as an administrative term.

events.title: Founding of IMRO
events.summary: The Internal Macedonian Revolutionary Organization was founded at Salonika to work for an autonomous Macedonia, its early leaders combining a Macedonian regional with a Bulgarian national identity.

events.title: The Ilinden Uprising
events.summary: On St Elijah's Day IMRO launched the Ilinden Uprising against Ottoman rule; though quickly crushed amid severe reprisals, it became a founding event of Macedonian national identity.

events.title: The Kruševo Republic
events.summary: During the uprising the insurgents proclaimed a short-lived republic in the town of Kruševo, which the Ottoman army overran after about ten days.

figures.name: Gotse Delchev
figures.blurb: One of the most important leaders of the Internal Macedonian Revolutionary Organization, Delchev sought to broaden the movement beyond its Bulgarian core toward an autonomous Macedonia; he was killed shortly before the Ilinden Uprising of 1903.

figures.name: Damyan Gruev
figures.blurb: A teacher and one of the founders of IMRO in 1893, Gruev was among the chief organisers of the revolutionary movement that mounted the Ilinden Uprising against Ottoman rule.

pullquote.text: Ilinden became a founding myth of Macedonian nationalism, even as the identity of the region's people remained contested among its neighbours.
pullquote.attribution: On the legacy of the 1903 uprising

eras.title: Partition, 'Vardar Macedonia' and the Yugoslav Republic
eras.standfirst: The Balkan Wars divided Macedonia among its neighbours; the Vardar portion passed to Serbia and Yugoslavia, where after 1945 a Macedonian republic, language and nation were formally established.
body: The Balkan Wars of 1912 and 1913 ended Ottoman rule and partitioned historic Macedonia among the victorious states. The greater share of the Aegean south went to Greece, a smaller eastern portion (Pirin Macedonia) to Bulgaria, and the northern Vardar valley — the territory of the present republic — to Serbia. 'Vardar Macedonia', as it became known, was incorporated into Serbia and, after the First World War, into the new Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes, later renamed Yugoslavia, where it was administered as 'South Serbia' and its distinct Slavic identity officially discouraged.

The Second World War brought occupation and partition once more, chiefly by Bulgaria, and a Communist-led Partisan resistance. The decisive turn for Macedonian nationhood came as the war ended. Within Josip Broz Tito's federal vision, the Anti-Fascist Assembly for the National Liberation of Macedonia met in 1944 and proclaimed a Macedonian state, and in 1945 the People's (later Socialist) Republic of Macedonia was constituted as one of the six republics of socialist Yugoslavia.

Within Yugoslavia the Macedonian nation was given institutional form for the first time. A standard Macedonian literary language was codified in 1945, an autocephalous Macedonian Orthodox Church was later proclaimed, and Skopje grew into a republican capital, rebuilt after a devastating earthquake in 1963. For four and a half decades Macedonia developed as a constituent republic of the Yugoslav federation, its national institutions maturing in a way that would make peaceful independence possible when Yugoslavia broke apart.

events.title: The Balkan Wars and the partition of Macedonia
events.summary: The Balkan Wars ended Ottoman rule and divided historic Macedonia among Greece, Serbia and Bulgaria; the northern Vardar region passed to Serbia, becoming the core of the future republic.

events.title: Vardar Macedonia within Yugoslavia
events.summary: After the First World War the Vardar region was incorporated into the Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes (later Yugoslavia), where it was administered as part of Serbia and its Slavic identity discouraged.

events.title: Proclamation of a Macedonian state (ASNOM)
events.summary: The Anti-Fascist Assembly for the National Liberation of Macedonia met on St Elijah's Day 1944 and proclaimed a Macedonian state within a future Yugoslav federation.

events.title: Socialist Republic of Macedonia and the codified language
events.summary: A People's (later Socialist) Republic of Macedonia was constituted as one of Yugoslavia's six republics, and a standard Macedonian literary language was codified for the first time.

events.title: The Skopje earthquake
events.summary: A powerful earthquake devastated Skopje, killing more than a thousand people; the city was rebuilt with international aid as the modern capital of the republic.

figures.name: Josip Broz Tito
figures.blurb: The Partisan commander and lifelong leader of socialist Yugoslavia, Tito built a federation of six republics in which a Socialist Republic of Macedonia, with its own recognised nation and language, took shape after 1945.

figures.name: Krste Misirkov
figures.blurb: A scholar and publicist, Misirkov argued early in the twentieth century for a distinct Macedonian literary language and national consciousness, anticipating the codification of standard Macedonian that followed in 1945.

pullquote.text: Most people with a Macedonian identity refer to the region that constitutes North Macedonia as Vardar Macedonia, the Greek part as Aegean Macedonia, and the Bulgarian part as Pirin Macedonia.
pullquote.attribution: On the partition of the region after 1913

eras.title: Independence and the Name Dispute with Greece
eras.standfirst: Independence came peacefully in 1991, but for nearly three decades the new state was blocked by Greece's objection to its name, until the Prespa Agreement renamed it North Macedonia.
body: As Yugoslavia disintegrated, Macedonia chose a different path from its neighbours. In a referendum held on 8 September 1991 the citizens voted overwhelmingly for sovereignty and independence, and under President Kiro Gligorov the republic seceded without the wars that engulfed Croatia, Bosnia and later Kosovo. But the new state immediately ran into a dispute with Greece, which objected that the name 'Macedonia' implied a territorial claim on its own northern province and an appropriation of the heritage of ancient Macedon. Greece blocked recognition, and in 1993 the country was admitted to the United Nations only under the provisional name 'the former Yugoslav Republic of Macedonia'.

To stabilise relations the two states signed an Interim Accord on 13 September 1995, under which Greece undertook not to object to the country's membership of international organisations referred to by the provisional name. When Greece nonetheless blocked an invitation to join NATO at the Bucharest summit of 2008, the matter went to the International Court of Justice, which ruled on 5 December 2011 that Greece had breached its obligation under Article 11 of the Interim Accord by objecting to the applicant's admission to NATO. Even so, the underlying dispute remained, freezing the country's progress toward both NATO and the European Union for years.

Resolution finally came through direct negotiation. On 17 June 2018, beside Lake Prespa, the two governments signed the Prespa Agreement, formally the 'Final Agreement for the Settlement of the Differences', under which the country's constitutional name became the 'Republic of North Macedonia', to be used erga omnes. The accord terminated the 1995 Interim Accord and explicitly distinguished the modern South Slavic Macedonian language and nationality from the ancient Hellenic civilization of Macedonia claimed by Greece. After ratification and constitutional amendment, the new name entered into force in February 2019, ending one of the longest-running disputes in modern European diplomacy.

events.title: Independence referendum
events.summary: Citizens voted by some 96 percent for independence, and the republic seceded peacefully from Yugoslavia under President Kiro Gligorov, alone among the Yugoslav republics in avoiding war.

events.title: UN admission as 'FYROM'
events.summary: Owing to Greece's objection over the name, the country was admitted to the United Nations under the provisional designation 'the former Yugoslav Republic of Macedonia'.

events.title: The Interim Accord with Greece
events.summary: Greece and the country signed an Interim Accord under which Greece agreed not to object to its membership of international bodies referred to by the provisional name.

events.title: ICJ rules against Greece
events.summary: The International Court of Justice held that Greece had breached the Interim Accord by objecting to the country's admission to NATO at the 2008 Bucharest summit.

events.title: The Prespa Agreement
events.summary: Greece and the country signed the Prespa Agreement, renaming it the Republic of North Macedonia (used erga omnes), terminating the 1995 accord and distinguishing the modern Slavic state from ancient Macedon.

figures.name: Kiro Gligorov
figures.blurb: A veteran politician who led the republic to a peaceful, negotiated independence from Yugoslavia in 1991, Gligorov steered the young state through its early years and the opening of the name dispute with Greece.

figures.name: Zoran Zaev
figures.role: Prime Minister and signatory of the Prespa Agreement
figures.blurb: As prime minister, Zaev negotiated and signed the 2018 Prespa Agreement with Greece, accepting the name 'North Macedonia' to unlock the country's NATO and EU prospects, a politically costly compromise.

pullquote.text: The official name shall be the 'Republic of North Macedonia', which shall be the constitutional name and shall be used erga omnes.

eras.title: The Ohrid Framework, NATO and the Road to Europe
eras.standfirst: An ethnic Albanian insurgency was settled by the Ohrid Framework Agreement; renamed, the country joined NATO in 2020, while a dispute with Bulgaria still complicates its European Union bid.
body: Independence did not free the young state from internal strain. Its large ethnic Albanian minority pressed for greater rights, and in early 2001 an insurgency by the ethnic Albanian National Liberation Army, drawing on veterans of the neighbouring Kosovo conflict, brought the country to the edge of civil war. With European Union and United States mediation the fighting was ended by the Ohrid Framework Agreement, signed on 13 August 2001. It rejected any territorial solution and reshaped the state as a civic, multi-ethnic one: it provided for decentralisation, equitable representation of communities in public administration, official use of a minority language such as Albanian where speakers exceed a fifth of the population, and 'double-majority' (Badinter) voting to protect minority interests on sensitive legislation.

With the name dispute resolved by the Prespa Agreement, the country's long-blocked Euro-Atlantic ambitions could advance. Greece lifted its objection, and after the allies signed an accession protocol in 2019 and all member parliaments ratified it, the Republic of North Macedonia deposited its instrument of accession in Washington and joined NATO as its thirtieth member on 27 March 2020. The country, a candidate for European Union membership since 2005, had long sought to anchor itself in Western institutions, and NATO entry marked the first great reward of the Prespa compromise.

The path into the European Union, however, ran into a fresh obstacle. From 2020 Bulgaria blocked the opening of EU accession talks, raising disputes over history, language and identity — including the contention that the Macedonian language and nation derive from Bulgarian — that touched the country's sense of itself. A French-brokered compromise allowed the formal opening of accession negotiations on 19 July 2022, but Bulgaria's conditions, including constitutional recognition of a Bulgarian minority, continued to complicate the process. North Macedonia thus entered the 2020s a member of NATO and an EU candidate, its statehood secure but its full integration into Europe still entangled in the unresolved legacies of the Macedonian Question.

events.title: The Ohrid Framework Agreement
events.summary: The agreement ended the 2001 ethnic Albanian insurgency, rejecting territorial partition and granting the Albanian minority greater rights through decentralisation, equitable representation, language rights and double-majority voting.

events.title: EU candidate status
events.summary: Having applied for membership in 2004, the country was granted European Union candidate status in December 2005, opening a long road toward accession.

events.title: North Macedonia joins NATO
events.summary: After the Prespa Agreement lifted Greece's objection, North Macedonia deposited its instrument of accession and became NATO's thirtieth member on 27 March 2020.

events.title: Bulgaria blocks EU accession talks
events.summary: Bulgaria vetoed the opening of EU accession negotiations over disputes about history, language and identity, including claims that Macedonian derives from Bulgarian.

events.title: EU accession negotiations open
events.summary: Following a French-brokered compromise, North Macedonia formally opened European Union accession negotiations, though Bulgaria's conditions continued to complicate the process.

figures.name: Ali Ahmeti
figures.blurb: Leader of the ethnic Albanian National Liberation Army during the 2001 insurgency, Ahmeti turned to politics after the Ohrid Framework Agreement, founding a party that became a long-standing partner in the country's coalition governments.

figures.name: Boris Trajkovski
figures.blurb: President of the republic during the 2001 conflict, Trajkovski helped steer the country toward the Ohrid Framework Agreement and its Euro-Atlantic course before his death in an air crash in 2004.

pullquote.text: North Macedonia is now part of the NATO family, a family of thirty nations and almost one billion people.
pullquote.attribution: NATO Secretary General Jens Stoltenberg, 27 March 2020

figures.name: Saint Clement of Ohrid
figures.role: Founder of the Ohrid literary school
figures.blurb: A disciple of Saints Cyril and Methodius who built Ohrid into a centre of Slavonic learning and Christianity, one of the founders of medieval Slavic literature and the region's enduring patron saint.

figures.name: Tsar Samuel
figures.blurb: Ruler of a powerful western Balkan state with its capital at Ohrid, Samuel resisted Byzantium for decades until his defeat by Basil II in 1014, after which the region was reabsorbed into the Byzantine Empire.

figures.name: Gotse Delchev
figures.blurb: A leading figure of the Internal Macedonian Revolutionary Organization, associated with the struggle for an autonomous Macedonia and the Ilinden Uprising of 1903; revered across the region, his legacy is claimed by both North Macedonia and Bulgaria.

figures.name: Kiro Gligorov
figures.blurb: Architect of the country's peaceful independence from Yugoslavia in 1991 and its first president, who guided the young state through the early years of the name dispute with Greece.
